15: A direct revelation: 'You killed the Author of life, whom God raised from the dead.' Archēgon tēs zōēs (pioneer/author of life) is one of Acts' most original Christological titles. The paradox is maximal: the one who is the source and pioneer of life was put to death. The resurrection is not merely resuscitation but the vindication of Christ's identity — if God raised him, God endorses his claims. Peter's speech invites repentance not as moral self-improvement but as a re-orientation toward the one they killed, now established as the sole mediator of life.
1: The healing at the Beautiful Gate is contextually a sign-act: it demonstrates that the kingdom power Christ exercised in his ministry (healing the lame, Luke 13:11-17; Acts 7:22) continues through the Spirit-filled apostles in his name. Peter and John's temple attendance at the hour of prayer shows they remain observant Jews; the healing will happen in the center of Israel's worship life, echoing Jesus's own signs in the temple precincts (Luke 19:45-21:37).
2: The man lame from birth (chōlos ek koilias mētros autou) being carried to the gate to ask alms establishes his life history: never walked, always dependent, positioned at the threshold of the holy space but excluded from full participation (Leviticus 21:18 excluded those with physical defects from priestly service; Qumran excluded the maimed from the eschatological community, 1QSa 2.5-7). His healing will fulfill the eschatological sign of Isaiah 35:6 — 'the lame man leaps like a deer'.
3: The lame man asks Peter and John for alms — he expects money, not healing. The Christological pattern: Jesus regularly exceeded the petitioner's request or expectation (the blind Bartimaeus asking for mercy, receiving sight; the paralytic at Bethesda expecting stirred water, receiving a healing word). The apostolic healing in Christ's name will similarly exceed what the man asked for, demonstrating that Christ's power is not bounded by human expectation or request.
4: 'Peter directed his gaze at him, as did John, and said, 'Look at us'' (atenisas de Petros eis auton syn tō Iōannē eipen blepson eis hēmas) — the deliberate, focused attention before the healing word echoes Jesus's healings where touch or attention preceded the act. The command 'look at us' redirects the lame man's gaze from the alms he expected. The Christological dynamic: before the name of Jesus is invoked, the man must attend to the bearers of Christ's authority.
5: 'He fixed his attention on them, expecting to receive something from them' — the expectation is economic, the reception will be existential. The Christological irony is consistent: people approach Jesus and his representatives seeking material benefit (bread, money, security) and encounter the infinitely greater gift of restoration to God's image. The man's economic desperation ('expecting something') will be transformed into leaping praise (Acts 3:8) — the Christological economy always gives more than asked.
6: 'I have no silver and gold, but what I do have I give to you. In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, rise up and walk!' — the explicit invocation of Christ's name as the healing authority is the Christological pivot. Peter has nothing from himself; the healing authority belongs entirely to 'Jesus Christ of Nazareth' — the title preserves the historical particularity (Nazareth, the 'despised' city, John 1:46) alongside the Christological claim (Christ = Messiah). The name spoken in command is the same name that will be declared 'the only name by which we must be saved' (Acts 4:12).
7: 'And he took him by the right hand and raised him up, and immediately his feet and ankles were made strong' — the physical contact ('took by the right hand and raised', ēgeiren) uses the same verb as the resurrection: Jesus 'raised up' (ēgeiren) is reflected in Peter 'raising' the lame man. The Christological embodiment: the apostle's act of lifting mirrors Christ's resurrection-lifting. The healing is not merely therapeutic but eschatologically symbolic — the resurrection power of the risen Christ manifests in the healing of every broken body it touches.
8: 'Leaping up he stood and began to walk, and entered the temple with them, walking and leaping and praising God' — the fulfillment of Isaiah 35:6 ('the lame man leaps like a deer') and Isaiah 35:2 LXX (the lame will enter the sanctuary). The man who had never walked enters the temple — the space from which his condition had excluded him — leaping and praising God. The Christological sign: Christ's kingdom reverses the exclusions of the old order; those kept at the threshold are brought in leaping.
9: 'All the people saw him walking and praising God' — the public, visible character of the healing is deliberate. The apostolic signs are not private experiences but publicly verifiable events. The Christological apologetics of Acts consistently emphasize public witness: 'this has not been done in a corner' (Acts 26:26). The healed man's exuberant walking through the temple courts is the apostolic sign's own proclamation before Peter says a word.
10: 'They recognized him as the one who sat at the Beautiful Gate asking for alms, and they were filled with wonder and amazement at what had happened to him' — recognition is the prerequisite for the sign's apologetic force. The crowd knows this man — he was a permanent fixture at the gate. Their amazement is the Spirit-created preparation for Peter's sermon. The Christological pattern: signs create the audience; proclamation provides the interpretation. Neither is sufficient without the other.
11: 'While he clung to Peter and John, all the people, utterly astounded, ran together to them in Solomon's Portico' — Solomon's Portico (the eastern colonnade of the temple courts) was a known gathering point (John 10:23 — Jesus walked there; Acts 5:12 — the apostles gathered there). The Christological use of the temple space: Jesus himself taught in Solomon's Portico; now his apostles, in his name, draw the same crowds. The continuity between Jesus's Jerusalem ministry and the apostolic ministry is geographic as well as theological.
12: Peter immediately redirects attention: 'Men of Israel, why do you wonder at this, or why do you stare at us, as though by our own power or piety we have made him walk?' — the Christological humility is strategic. The temptation in miraculous signs is to divert attention to the instrument. Peter's deflection is the pattern of all authentic Christological ministry: the apostle points away from himself to Christ. The healing was 'by faith in his name' (Acts 3:16) — Christ remains the agent even when apostles are the instruments.
13: 'The God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, the God of our fathers, glorified his servant Jesus' — Peter's Christological proclamation in the temple begins by embedding Jesus within Israel's covenant history. The fourfold patriarchal identification establishes that the God who glorified Jesus is Israel's covenant God, not a foreign deity. The 'servant' (pais) identification connects Jesus to the Isaianic Servant (Isaiah 42:1; Acts 52:13; Acts 53:11) — the one whom the God of Israel appointed for the salvation of Israel and the nations.
14: 'But you denied the Holy and Righteous One (ton hagion kai dikaion), and asked for a murderer to be granted to you' — the titles 'Holy One' and 'Righteous One' are the Christological antitheses of what the crowd chose: they preferred Barabbas (a murderer) over the Holy and Righteous One. The double title carries OT weight: 'Holy One' is a divine name (Isaiah 6:3; Acts 43:3); 'Righteous One' (dikaios) is the Servant's designation (Isaiah 53:11). Peter is declaring that the one they rejected bore the titles that defined the object of Israel's hope.
16: 'And his name — by faith in his name — has made this man strong whom you see and know, and the faith that is through Jesus has given the man this perfect health in the presence of you all.' The healing is explicitly mediated through 'his name' and 'faith in his name' and 'faith through Jesus' — three iterations of the same Christological point. The name of the risen Jesus is not a magical formula but the invocation of the living person whose authority over creation persists through resurrection. The 'perfect health' (holoklērian, complete wholeness) is the total restoration that the name of Jesus accomplishes.
17: 'Now, brothers, I know that you acted in ignorance, as did also your rulers' — the Christological mercy: Peter offers his audience the same amnesty that Jesus extended from the cross ('Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do', Luke 23:34). The category of ignorance-sin carries the OT provision for inadvertent transgression (Lev 5; Numbers 15:27-31). The crucifixion was the greatest crime in history; the offer of repentance and forgiveness for it is the greatest mercy in history — only possible because Christ bore the consequence.
18: 'But what God foretold by the mouth of all the prophets, that his Christ would suffer, he thus fulfilled' — the 'necessary suffering' of the Christ is the interpretive key to the OT. 'All the prophets' is comprehensive: not selected proof-texts but the entire prophetic witness to the Messiah's fate. Peter's claim is that a suffering Christ is not a Christian invention but the consistent message of Israel's prophets — the scandal is that Israel's own Scripture predicted what Israel could not accept. The Christological hermeneutic of Acts reads all prophecy toward the suffering-and-risen Messiah.
19: 'Repent therefore, and turn back (epistrepsate), that your sins may be blotted out' — the invitation to repentance is structured as a return: epistrephō (turn back/return) is the LXX verb for Israel's return to YHWH in the prophets (Malachi 3:7; Isaiah 44:22; Jeremiah 3:7). The Christological content of repentance: turning toward Christ is the new form of turning toward YHWH, because God has now defined himself definitively in Christ. The blotting out of sins (exaleiphthēnai, completely erased) is the new covenant fulfillment of Isaiah 43:25 and Acts 44:22.
20: 'That times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord' — the eschatological promise tied to repentance is 'times of refreshing' (kairoi anapsyxeōs, seasons of rest/breathing space). The Christological hope: when Israel repents and turns to Christ, the fullness of eschatological blessing follows. The sending of 'the Christ appointed for you, Jesus' (Acts 3:20b) points to the parousia as the telos of Israel's repentance. The mission to Israel is not merely evangelistic but eschatologically freighted.
21: 'Whom heaven must receive until the time for restoring all the things about which God spoke by the mouth of his holy prophets long ago' — Christ's present session in heaven ('heaven must receive him') is the condition of the 'universal restoration' (apokatastaseōs pantōn) still outstanding. The Christological eschatology of Acts: Christ reigns now but the full restoration awaits his return. Peter's preaching in the temple envisions Israel's repentance as the precondition for the parousia, consistent with Paul's theology in Romans 11:25-26.
22: 'Moses said, 'The Lord God will raise up for you a prophet like me from your brothers. You shall listen to him in whatever he tells you'' — the Deuteronomy 18:15-18 promise of the Prophet-like-Moses is Peter's OT climax for the sermon. The Christological typology: Jesus recapitulates the Moses pattern (deliverer, mediator, lawgiver, miracle-worker) but at a cosmic scale. The command 'listen to him' echoes the Transfiguration's heavenly voice (Luke 9:35 — 'This is my Son, my Chosen One; listen to him!'). Moses points to and demands attention to his greater successor.
23: 'And it shall be that every soul who does not listen to that prophet shall be destroyed from the people' — the urgency of the Christological claim: failure to heed the Prophet-like-Moses carries covenant consequences. The warning is not new (Deuteronomy 18:19 — God himself will require it); Peter applies it to rejection of Jesus. The Christological exclusivity is not arbitrary: Moses himself foresaw that the ultimate Prophet would require ultimate allegiance. To reject Jesus is to reject the one Moses pointed toward and warned against rejecting.
24: 'And all the prophets who have spoken, from Samuel and those who came after him, also proclaimed these days' — Peter's claim is that all prophetic history, from Samuel onward, pointed to 'these days' — the days of Christ's coming, suffering, resurrection, and the Spirit's outpouring. The Christological reading of the whole prophetic canon is programmatic for Acts: the prophets were not merely recording history but previewing the Christ-event. The arrival of 'these days' is the fulfillment that all prophecy was building toward.
25: 'You are the sons of the prophets and of the covenant that God made with your fathers, saying to Abraham, 'And in your offspring shall all the families of the earth be blessed'' — the Abrahamic promise (Genesis 12:3; Acts 22:18) cited as the broadest frame for the gospel's universal scope. Paul cites this same text in Galatians 3:8 and identifies 'your offspring' as Christ (Galatians 3:16 — singular sperma). The Christological seed of Abraham through whom all nations are blessed is Jesus; the blessing going to 'all the families of the earth' is the Gentile mission that Acts will narrate.
26: 'God, having raised up his servant, sent him to you first, to bless you by turning every one of you from your wickedness' — the resurrection-sending connects directly to the Abrahamic promise: Christ raised is Christ sent. The 'first' priority for the Jewish audience is mission strategy (Romans 1:16 — 'to the Jew first and also to the Greek'), not ethnic privilege. The blessing Christ brings — 'turning every one of you from your wickedness' — is the Christological fulfillment of the covenant promise: in the seed of Abraham, wickedness is turned around, the blessing flows. The sermon closes where the gospel's trajectory begins: with Christ as the Servant-Messiah sent by Israel's God to bless the world.
